payload.title: Prioritize SQLite-in-Git resolution via canonical mutation evidence
payload.summary: User and architect converged on a preferred direction for the SQLite-in-Git problem: do not make raw SQLite file merge the primary invariant. Instead, make governed mutations emit canonical, mergeable operation evidence; treat SQLite as local runtime substrate/projection that can be rebuilt or reconciled from those admitted operations.
evidence: Problem pressure arose from Git-backed Narada User Sites and task machinery. Options considered included single-authority SQLite, raw SQL-level reconciliation, Git lease files, branch-per-device admission, avoiding SQLite in Git, and event/delta based projection rebuild. The preferred near-term compromise is SQLite for ergonomic local runtime plus Git-tracked canonical mutation records for merge/replay/reconciliation.
proposal: Make this a priority design/implementation arc: define canonical mutation records for SQLite-backed Site/task/inbox state; require every governed mutation to emit an operation/event/envelope; make Git carry mergeable mutation evidence; provide checkout/pull reconcile or rebuild commands that bring SQLite into agreement by replaying admitted operations. Raw SQL-level DB merging may remain a repair/migration tool, not the governing invariant.
payload.recommendation: Adopt the doctrine: SQLite is the fast local runtime substrate; Git carries canonical mutation evidence; reconciliation happens by replaying admitted operations, not by merging opaque SQLite files. Prioritize guards so no governed DB mutation can occur without its exported canonical delta.

promotion.note: Envelope archived; no target-zone mutation was performed.
